feedback.text: One of the most pressing and overlooked unfair commercial practices in todays digital marketplace is the revocation or disabling of digital products that consumers have paid forparticularly in the video game industry. When consumers purchase a digital product like a video game, they expect to retain access to it permanently, just as they would with a physical product. However, many games today are sold as live services or come with online dependencies that allow publishers to later render them unusableeven for single-player contentwithout offering alternatives or compensation. This is often done with no legal obligation to preserve access or inform consumers at the time of purchase. In practice, this turns purchases into temporary rentals disguised as ownership. This is not only misleading, but also deeply unfair and exploitative. It undermines the fundamental principle of commerce: that a purchase grants the buyer lasting rights over the product. Worse, consumers are often told after the fact that "ownership is outdated" and that they must "get used to not owning digital goods"a narrative pushed by large corporations to normalize consumer disempowerment and reduce accountability. I strongly believe that the EU must consider concrete laws that: Clarify and protect digital ownership rights for consumers; Prevent companies from unilaterally revoking access to purchased content; Require clear and enforceable end-of-life plans for digital products that rely on online infrastructure; Enforce transparency at the point of sale regarding the nature of digital purchases (ownership vs. license, online requirement, risk of future loss). This issue primarily affects video games due to their growing reliance on online components and the size of the market, but the underlying principle applies to all forms of digital media: music, film, books, and software. Consumers are increasingly at the mercy of centralized platforms and service models that offer no guarantees of continued accesseven when no technical reason exists for the shutdown. The right to retain what we pay for is fundamental to fair commerce. If digital markets are allowed to evolve without protections for consumers, we risk normalizing a system where companies can sell access without responsibility, and take back content at will. That is not fairnessit is exploitation. Thank you for addressing digital fairness at a structural level. I hope you will seriously consider digital ownership and end-of-life rights as part of this initiative.
